perturbation.original_text: (c) "Contract Territory" shall mean the entire world.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) "Contract Territory" shall mean the United States of America.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the scope of the contract geographically. However, the contract later mentions specific compensation related to Australia and New Zealand in section 11, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: By explicitly defining 'Contract Territory' as the United States but later referencing 'Royalty Compensation' related to sales in Australia and New Zealand, the contract creates an internal inconsistency regarding the scope of the agreement. This makes it unclear if the royalty provisions are enforceable, as they apply to sales outside the defined territory, which is problematic under general contract law regarding consideration and scope.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'entire world' to 'United States of America' as the Contract Territory, while later sections of the contract reference royalty compensation for sales in Australia and New Zealand, creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that this 'creates an internal inconsistency regarding the scope of the agreement,' making it 'unclear if the royalty provisions are enforceable.' This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the term 'Contract Territory' is explicitly defined but its application is contradicted by subsequent provisions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 28. GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia applicable to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed within the Commonwealth of Virginia and, in the event of any litigation arising out of this Agreement, venue shall be the Commonwealth of Virginia.
perturbation.changed_text: 28. GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Isle of Jersey applicable to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed within the Isle of Jersey and, in the event of any litigation arising out of this Agreement, venue shall be the Isle of Jersey.
perturbation.explanation: This clause initially specified Virginia law, but now says the contract is under Isle of Jersey law. This is problematic because one party, Teardrop Putter Corporation, is located in South Carolina and the contract will not be performed in the Isle of Jersey.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles - U.S. Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the Isle of Jersey, when the contract has significant connections to the United States (South Carolina company, US PGA tour references, US Dollar payments), contradicts standard choice-of-law principles in US contract law. Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that the chosen law must have a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction. Applying Isle of Jersey law when the primary activities and parties are US-based could render the choice-of-law clause unenforceable, leading to legal uncertainty and potentially contradicting US laws that would otherwise apply.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Virginia to the Isle of Jersey creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that the chosen law must have a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction. The Perturbation Explanation states that one party is in South Carolina and the contract will not be performed in the Isle of Jersey. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' Because the altered text applies Isle of Jersey law when the primary activities and parties are US-based, this makes the choice-of-law clause unenforceable.
